blocks.text: Time is one of Allah's most precious gifts, and Salah is the mechanism by which we sanctify it. By anchoring our day to five specific intervals, we ensure that no matter how deep we sink into worldly affairs, we are repeatedly pulled back to our spiritual center. These timings are not arbitrary; they follow the natural cycles of the sun, weaving the remembrance of Allah into the very fabric of the cosmos.

Fajr begins at the first light of dawn and ends at sunrise. Dhuhr starts after the sun passes its zenith (the highest point). Asr begins when the shadow of an object is equal to its length (or twice its length in the Hanafi school) and lasts until sunset. Maghrib begins immediately after sunset and ends when the red twilight fades. Isha begins when the twilight has disappeared and lasts until the middle of the night (or until Fajr in cases of necessity). These intervals ensure that we are never far from a moment of reconnection.

blocks.textUthmani: فَإِذَا قَضَيۡتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَٱذۡكُرُواْ ٱللَّهَ قِيَٰمٗا وَقُعُودٗا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِكُمۡۚ فَإِذَا ٱطۡمَأۡنَنتُمۡ فَأَقِيمُواْ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَۚ إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ كَانَتۡ عَلَى ٱلۡمُؤۡمِنِينَ كِتَٰبٗا مَّوۡقُوتٗا
blocks.translationEn: After performing the ritual prayer, continue to remember God- standing, sitting, and lying on your sides- and once you are safe, keep up regular prayer, for prayer is obligatory for the believers at prescribed times

blocks.text: Allah states clearly that prayer has been decreed for the believers at fixed times. This 'Kitaban Mawquta' (a decree of fixed times) teaches us discipline and consistency. It reminds us that we do not pray when we 'feel like it' or when it is convenient, but when Allah has invited us to meet Him. This structure reorients our entire schedule around our Creator rather than fitting our Creator into our schedule.

blocks.textEn: It was narrated from 'Abdullah bin 'Amr that the Prophet (ﷺ) said:"When you pray Fajr, its time is until the first part of the sun appears. When you pray Zuhr, its time is until 'Asr comes. When you pray 'Asr, its time is until the sun turns yellow. When you pray Maghrib, its time is until the twilight has disappeared. When you pray 'Isha, its time is until half of the night has passed

blocks.text: This detailed hadith, narrated by Abdullah ibn Amr, provides the precise astronomical markers for each prayer time. The Prophet ﷺ taught us how to observe the movement of the sun and the changing of light to determine when to stand before Allah. By learning these signs, we develop a deeper connection to the creation and a greater awareness of the passage of time as a limited resource for worship.

The best time for any prayer is at the beginning of its window. Delaying the prayer without a valid reason is discouraged, as it shows a lack of eagerness to meet Allah. Try to anticipate the next prayer time so you are already in a state of Wudu and readiness when the Adhan is called.

The spacing of the five prayers is a mercy. Fajr starts your day with light and purpose. Dhuhr breaks the intensity of the workday. Asr provides a mid-afternoon spiritual boost. Maghrib marks the transition to the evening. Isha concludes your day in a state of peace and submission. This rhythm prevents the heart from becoming hard and the soul from becoming forgetful.

Understanding the astronomical basis of these times helps us appreciate the order of Allah's universe. When you stand for prayer at its prescribed time, you are aligning yourself with the natural rhythm of creation. This alignment is a powerful aid in achieving Khushu, as it reminds you that you are part of a much larger, divine order.
